title: Psychedelic Research and Kundalini: What MAPS and Johns Hopkins Found

body:
Psychedelic Research and Kundalini: What MAPS and Johns Hopkins Found

You're here because you're probably freaking out. Maybe you took something - mushrooms, acid, MDMA - and now your world's been cracked open in a way that feels less like enlightenment and more like a never-ending panic attack. Or maybe you've been doing some deep spiritual work, meditation, breathwork, and suddenly you're feeling things, seeing things, hearing things that don't make any sense. You're wondering if you broke your brain, if you're going crazy. I get it. I've been there. That 3 AM terror, the feeling of being utterly alone with something too vast and terrifying to name. It's a special kind of hell, isn't it? Like your soul's being peeled open, and you can't stop it.

You've probably heard about kundalini. Maybe someone mentioned it, or you stumbled across it online while frantically searching for answers. And now you're connecting the dots - the buzzing, the heat, the visions, the intense emotional swings - to this ancient concept of spiritual energy. You're also seeing all this hype about psychedelics, how they're supposed to be these miracle cures, these shortcuts to spiritual awakening. So, what's the deal? Do they connect? Is one causing the other? And more critically, what do the big guys - MAPS and Johns Hopkins - say about all this? Because you need answers, not just platitudes. You need someone to tell you what's real and what's not, and how to survive this.

Let me tell you straight up: this isn't some fluffy spiritual blog post. This is real talk from someone who walked through the fire and came out the other side. You're not alone, even if it feels like it right now. And no, you're probably not going crazy. You're experiencing something very real, very powerful, and sometimes, very destabilizing. It's often called a "spiritual emergency" or "kundalini awakening," and yeah, psychedelics can absolutely kickstart it, or intensify an already bubbling process. I've seen it countless times, and I've lived it. So let's talk about what the research says, and what it doesn't, about this wild ride you're on.

The Elephant in the Room: Psychedelics and Intense Spiritual Experiences

Look, psychedelics aren't new. Indigenous cultures have used them for millennia for healing, vision quests, and spiritual connection. But in the West, we've had this weird on-again, off-again relationship with them. Now, we're in an "on" phase, with organizations like MAPS (Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies) and Johns Hopkins leading the charge in researching their therapeutic potential. They're doing incredible work with MDMA for PTSD, psilocybin for depression and end-of-life anxiety, you know? It's exciting stuff, honestly, and it's bringing these substances back into the medical conversation.

But here's the thing: when you open the doors of perception with these substances, you're not just getting a nice little spiritual high. You're poking at something ancient, something deep within your own consciousness. And sometimes, that "something" is kundalini. It's like you're tapping into the core operating system of your being, and if that system is buggy, or if you're not prepared for the download, things can get really messy, really fast. You might feel a surge of energy, intense heat, vibrations, involuntary movements - all classic signs of kundalini activity. You might have visions, hear voices, feel like you're dissolving or expanding into the universe. It can be ecstatic, terrifying, or both. Often, it's both.

The researchers at MAPS and Johns Hopkins aren't explicitly studying "kundalini awakenings" in the way a yogi might define them. Their focus is more on the therapeutic outcomes, the psychological and neurological changes. But they absolutely acknowledge and document deep spiritual experiences, mystical experiences, and what they sometimes call "challenging experiences." And many of these challenging experiences, when described, sound an awful lot like what people in spiritual traditions call kundalini crisis or spiritual emergency. They're just using different language to describe it. Does that land? Because it's important to understand they're observing the same phenomena, just through a different lens.

MAPS and Challenging Experiences

MAPS, particularly with their MDMA-assisted psychotherapy research, has been very open about the potential for difficult experiences. They don't sugarcoat it. They know that sometimes, processing deep trauma can be incredibly intense, even agonizing. They call these "challenging experiences," and they emphasize the importance of preparation and integration - having skilled therapists there to guide you through it. They're not saying "just take the pill and you'll be fine." They're saying "take the pill with trained support, in a safe setting, and prepare for a potentially wild ride."

When you read through their protocols, you see they're designed to help people process intense emotions, bodily sensations, and altered states of consciousness. Many of the symptoms of a kundalini crisis - the overwhelming anxiety, the sense of unreality, the physical discomfort, the existential dread - could easily fall under the umbrella of a "challenging experience" in their approach. They know you might feel like you're losing your mind. They know you might feel like you're dying. That's why their therapists are trained to sit with you, to hold space, to help you make sense of it. They don't call it kundalini, but they're dealing with the same raw material of human consciousness being stretched to its limits.

I've talked to people who went through MAPS trials, and they describe moments of intense energy surges, body tremors, and a feeling of being utterly overwhelmed. The difference is, they had professional guidance to help them integrate it. You, sitting there at 3 AM, might not have that. That's why you're searching, right? Because you're trying to figure out how to integrate something that feels like it blew up your life.

Johns Hopkins and Mystical Experiences

Johns Hopkins, especially with their psilocybin research, has focused a lot on the "mystical experience." They've even developed scales to measure it, to quantify something that feels by nature unquantifiable. And what they've found is that these mystical experiences - feelings of unity, sacredness, transcendence of time and space, deeply positive mood - are often correlated with lasting positive changes in mood and behavior. They're not just fleeting highs; they can be deeply life-changing.

But here's the kicker: even within these "mystical experiences," there can be intense, sometimes frightening moments. You might feel like you're dissolving into nothingness, or confronting your deepest fears. You might experience what some call an "ego death," where your sense of self temporarily vanishes. For some, this is liberating. For others, it's terrifying. And for someone experiencing an unmanaged kundalini awakening, that feeling of ego dissolution, that loss of self, can push them right into a full-blown spiritual emergency. It feels like you're literally disappearing, and your mind can't cope.

So yeah, while Johns Hopkins highlights the positive aspects of these experiences, they also acknowledge the intensity. They understand that messing with consciousness isn't always rainbows and unicorns. It can be incredibly disorienting. And when you factor in the body sensations, the energy movements - which they might not label as kundalini but are clearly present in participant reports - you start to see the overlap. They're exploring the same terrain, just with different maps.

The Missing Piece: Direct Kundalini Research

Here's the truth: neither MAPS nor Johns Hopkins are directly researching kundalini as a primary focus. They're not setting up experiments to see how psychedelics interact with kundalini energy systems. That's not their mandate, and it's not how Western science typically approaches these things. Kundalini is still largely viewed as a spiritual or esoteric concept, not a medically recognized phenomenon. So you won't find a study titled "Psilocybin and Kundalini Activation: A Randomized Controlled Trial." Not yet, anyway.

But that doesn't mean the connection isn't real. It just means it's not being studied in a way that fits neatly into their current research models. What their research *does* show is that psychedelics can induce incredibly powerful, sometimes overwhelming, spiritual and psychological states. And many of those states, when described by participants, bear a striking resemblance to what people undergoing kundalini awakening or crisis report. The intense energy, the involuntary movements, the visions, the emotional purging, the feeling of universal connection or dissolution - it's all there, just under different labels.

I'll tell you what, though. The people who are experiencing these things, like you, they don't care about the scientific labels. They care about understanding what's happening to them, and how to make it stop or at least manage it. They're looking for guidance, for validation, for a way out of the suffering. And that's where the gap is. Western science is slowly catching up to the reality of these experiences, but it's got a long way to go to fully integrate concepts like kundalini.

What This Means for You: Navigating the Aftermath

So, you've had a psychedelic experience, or you're deep into some spiritual practice, and now you feel like you're having a kundalini awakening or crisis. What do you do? The research from MAPS and Johns Hopkins, while not directly addressing kundalini, still offers some crucial takeaways that can help you. They highlight the importance of certain things, even if they don't use the same words.

  • Integration is Everything: This is probably the single most important lesson. Taking a psychedelic or having a spontaneous spiritual opening isn't a one-time event. The real work begins afterward. You've opened a door, and now you have to figure out what to do with everything that came through it. This means processing the experiences, understanding their meaning, and making changes in your life to align with what you've learned or experienced. Without integration, these experiences can be incredibly destabilizing. You're left with all this raw, powerful energy and no idea how to channel it.
  • Support, Support, Support: You shouldn't do this alone. The MAPS and Johns Hopkins models emphasize having trained guides. If you're in a kundalini crisis, you need support. This might mean finding a therapist who understands spiritual emergencies, a spiritual director, or a community that can hold space for you. You can't just tough this out. You're dealing with immense energies, and you need someone to help you ground yourself.
  • Grounding Practices: When you're feeling unmoored, when your body is buzzing and your mind is racing, you need to ground yourself. This means focusing on the physical world, on your body, on simple, everyday tasks. Go for a walk. Eat nourishing food. Take a warm bath. Spend time in nature. Limit stimulants. These aren't just "nice to haves"; they're essential for bringing you back down to earth when your system is overloaded.
  • Patience and Self-Compassion: This isn't a sprint; it's a marathon. You might feel like you're losing your mind, but you're probably not. You're going through a deep transformation. Be kind to yourself. You wouldn't expect a broken bone to heal overnight, right? This is an internal healing and reorganization that takes time, sometimes years. Don't beat yourself up for feeling scared or confused. It's a natural response to something so intense.

You're not crazy. You're undergoing something that very few people talk about openly. But it's real. And there are ways to deal with it. I've been there, remember? That feeling of being utterly alone, like no one could possibly understand. But people do. And there are resources. You just have to find them.

Where to Find Help and Understanding

Given that mainstream research isn't directly looking at kundalini, where do you turn? You look to the people who've been talking about it for decades, the ones who've been trying to bridge the gap between spiritual experiences and Western psychology. People like Stanislav and Christina Grof, who coined the term "spiritual emergency." Their work is invaluable. You should really check out their books, like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) and Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). They've documented countless cases of people going through exactly what you might be experiencing - intense, non-ordinary states of consciousness that are often misdiagnosed as psychosis.

Then there's the specific literature on kundalini. It's often written from a more spiritual perspective, but it can help you understand the energies you're feeling. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a classic, one of the first Western doctors to really explore this phenomenon. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is another excellent resource, offering practical advice for managing the process. These aren't going to give you a quick fix, but they'll give you context, validation, and some tools to start working with. Know what I mean?

Here's what you need to remember: you're not alone. What you're going through, however terrifying, is a recognized, albeit poorly understood, human experience. The work being done by MAPS and Johns Hopkins, even if it doesn't use the word "kundalini," is slowly but surely opening the door for a broader acceptance of these intense spiritual states within Western medicine. It's a slow process, but it's happening.

Your job right now is to survive, to learn, and to integrate. Find the support you need. Read the books. Practice grounding. Be patient with yourself. This isn't a breakdown; it's potentially a breakthrough, albeit a very messy one. You're strong enough to get through this. You wouldn't be here, searching for answers at 3 AM, if you weren't. Hold on. You've got this.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
